examples.explanation: Step 1: Count total non-obstacle cells = 8 (all except obstacles, none here). Start=(0,0), target=(2,2). Step 2: Initialize visited 3x4 grid all False. Step 3: Begin backtrack(0,0,0). Mark (0,0) visited, count=1. Explore 4 directions: down to (1,0) valid, right to (0,1) valid. Step 4: Branch 1: backtrack(1,0,1). Mark visited, count=2. Explore: down to (2,0) valid, up to (0,0) visited, right to (1,1) valid. Branch 1.1: backtrack(2,0,2). Mark, count=3. Explore: down out, up to (1,0) visited, right to (2,1) valid. Branch 1.1.1: backtrack(2,1,3). Mark, count=4. Explore: down out, up to (1,1) valid, right to (2,2) target but count=4 not total 8, left to (2,0) visited. Branch 1.1.1.1: backtrack(1,1,4). Mark, count=5. Continue exploring... Eventually one path: (0,0)->(1,0)->(2,0)->(2,1)->(1,1)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(0,3)->(1,3)->(2,3)->(2,2) visits all 8 cells, count increments to 1. Another path found similarly, total count=2.

Step 1: Total non-obstacle cells=9, start=(0,0), target=(2,2). Step 2: Initialize visited 3x3 grid. Step 3: backtrack(0,0,0). Mark (0,0), count=1. Explore directions. Multiple valid paths exist. Example path 1: (0,0)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(1,2)->(2,2) but only visits 5 cells, not valid. Valid path must visit all 9 cells. One valid path: (0,0)->(1,0)->(2,0)->(2,1)->(1,1)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(1,2)->(2,2) visits all cells. Another: (0,0)->(0,1)->(1,1)->(2,1)->(2,0)->(1,0)->(1,2)->(0,2)->(2,2). After exploring all possibilities, 4 distinct paths visit all cells exactly once.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Backtracking O(4^(m*n))
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths in the grid using backtracking, checking each cell's four directions recursively. It counts a path only if it visits every non-obstacle cell exactly once and ends at the target.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Unique Paths III with Backtracking
ladders.desc: Complete implementation counting all paths visiting every non-obstacle cell exactly once from start to end.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Unique Paths III problem solved using brute force backtracking. The intuition is to explore all possible paths in the grid using depth-first search, counting only those that visit every non-obstacle cell exactly once and end at the target cell. We treat the grid as a graph where each cell connects to its four neighbors (up, down, left, right). The algorithm starts at the given starting cell, recursively explores all four directions, marking visited cells to avoid cycles. A path is valid only if: 1) it ends at the target cell, and 2) the number of visited cells equals the total number of non-obstacle cells. The brute force nature comes from exploring all 4^(m*n) possibilities in worst case. Step-by-step: 1) Count total non-obstacle cells. 2) Find start position. 3) Initialize visited grid. 4) Define recursive backtrack function that: checks bounds/obstacles/visited, marks current cell visited, if at target and visited all non-obstacle cells, increment count, else recursively explore four directions, unmarks cell on backtrack. 5) Call function from start. Time: O(4^(m*n)) worst case as each cell can branch 4 ways. Space: O(m*n) for recursion stack and visited grid. Connection to L1: This uses the backtracking subroutine as its core recursive engine. Common mistakes: Not unmarking cells on backtrack (prevents exploring alternative paths), incorrect visited count comparison, missing obstacle checks. Real code pattern: def uniquePathsIII(grid): m,n=len(grid),len(grid[0]); total=0; start=None; for i in range(m): for j in range(n): if grid[i][j]==0: total+=1; elif grid[i][j]==1: start=(i,j); total+=1; elif grid[i][j]==2: target=(i,j); total+=1; visited=[[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; count=0; def backtrack(i,j,visited_count): nonlocal count; if not (0<=i<m and 0<=j<n) or grid[i][j]==-1 or visited[i][j]: return; visited_count+=1; visited[i][j]=True; if (i,j)==target and visited_count==total: count+=1; else: for di,dj in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: backtrack(i+di,j+dj,visited_count); visited[i][j]=False; backtrack(start[0],start[1],0); return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Count total non-obstacle cells = 8 (all except obstacles, none here). Start=(0,0), target=(2,2). Step 2: Initialize visited 3x4 grid all False. Step 3: Begin backtrack(0,0,0). Mark (0,0) visited, count=1. Explore 4 directions: down to (1,0) valid, right to (0,1) valid. Step 4: Branch 1: backtrack(1,0,1). Mark visited, count=2. Explore: down to (2,0) valid, up to (0,0) visited, right to (1,1) valid. Branch 1.1: backtrack(2,0,2). Mark, count=3. Explore: down out, up to (1,0) visited, right to (2,1) valid. Branch 1.1.1: backtrack(2,1,3). Mark, count=4. Explore: down out, up to (1,1) valid, right to (2,2) target but count=4 not total 8, left to (2,0) visited. Branch 1.1.1.1: backtrack(1,1,4). Mark, count=5. Continue exploring... Eventually one path: (0,0)->(1,0)->(2,0)->(2,1)->(1,1)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(0,3)->(1,3)->(2,3)->(2,2) visits all 8 cells, count increments to 1. Another path found similarly, total count=2.

Step 1: Total non-obstacle cells=9, start=(0,0), target=(2,2). Step 2: Initialize visited 3x3 grid. Step 3: backtrack(0,0,0). Mark (0,0), count=1. Explore directions. Multiple valid paths exist. Example path 1: (0,0)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(1,2)->(2,2) but only visits 5 cells, not valid. Valid path must visit all 9 cells. One valid path: (0,0)->(1,0)->(2,0)->(2,1)->(1,1)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(1,2)->(2,2) visits all cells. Another: (0,0)->(0,1)->(1,1)->(2,1)->(2,0)->(1,0)->(1,2)->(0,2)->(2,2). After exploring all possibilities, 4 distinct paths visit all cells exactly once.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking DFS on Grid
ladders.desc: Implement recursive backtracking to explore all paths from a start cell in a grid.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that performs depth-first search with backtracking on a grid. The intuition is to systematically explore all possible paths by moving to neighboring cells, marking visited cells to prevent cycles, and unmarking them when backtracking to explore alternative paths. This mimics walking through a maze where you try all routes, keeping track of where you've been. The algorithm: function backtrack(i, j, visited_count): 1) Check if (i,j) is within grid bounds, not an obstacle (-1), and not already visited. If invalid, return. 2) Mark cell (i,j) as visited and increment visited_count. 3) If current cell is the target and visited_count equals total non-obstacle cells, increment global path count. 4) Otherwise, recursively call backtrack on all four neighboring cells (i+1,j), (i-1,j), (i,j+1), (i,j-1). 5) Unmark cell (i,j) as visited (backtrack) before returning. Time complexity for this subroutine in isolation is O(4^k) where k is path length, but in full problem it's O(4^(m*n)). Space: O(m*n) for recursion depth and visited storage. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the full solution to explore paths. Connection to L2: This implements the core backtracking technique. Common mistakes: Forgetting to unmark cells (leads to incomplete exploration), incorrect base case order. Real code pattern: def backtrack(i,j,visited_count): if i<0 or i>=m or j<0 or j>=n or grid[i][j]==-1 or visited[i][j]: return; visited[i][j]=True; visited_count+=1; if (i,j)==target and visited_count==total: count+=1; else: for di,dj in directions: backtrack(i+di,j+dj,visited_count); visited[i][j]=False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize visited 2x2 all False. Step 2: backtrack(0,0,0). Mark (0,0) visited, count=1. Not at target. Explore 4 neighbors: down to (1,0) valid, right to (0,1) valid, up/left out. Step 3: Branch backtrack(1,0,1). Mark (1,0), count=2. Explore: down out, up to (0,0) visited, right to (1,1) target but count=2 not total 4, left out. So backtrack(1,1,2) called but returns without incrementing. Then unmark (1,0). Step 4: Back to (0,0), try right to (0,1). Mark (0,1), count=2. Explore similarly. Continue until all paths tried. Each full path visits all 4 cells: e.g., (0,0)->(1,0)->(1,1)->(0,1) but that visits only 3 cells, need to visit all 4, so paths like (0,0)->(1,0)->(1,1) invalid. Valid path: (0,0)->(1,0)->(0,0) invalid due to revisit. Actually with total=4, need to visit all cells, so path must be Hamiltonian. Example valid: (0,0)->(0,1)->(1,1)->(1,0) but ends at (1,0) not target. To end at (1,1), path must cover all cells ending at (1,1).

Step 1: Grid has obstacle at (0,1). Total non-obstacle cells=3. Step 2: backtrack(0,0,0). Mark (0,0), count=1. Explore neighbors: down to (1,0) valid, right to (0,1) obstacle, so invalid. Step 3: backtrack(1,0,1). Mark (1,0), count=2. Explore: down out, up to (0,0) visited, right to (1,1) target but count=2 not total 3, left out. So no valid path yet. Unmark (1,0). Back to (0,0), no other options. So no path visits all 3 cells ending at target.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking with Path Counting
ladders.desc: Implement backtracking that counts paths meeting specific visit conditions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core logic of backtracking with additional constraints: counting only paths that visit a specific number of cells and end at a target. The intuition is to extend simple DFS by keeping track of how many cells have been visited and checking a completion condition. Unlike standard DFS that just explores, this adds counting when a path satisfies: 1) reaches target cell, and 2) has visited exactly 'total' cells. This ensures we only count Hamiltonian paths (visiting each cell exactly once) from start to target. The algorithm: Use recursion to explore paths, maintain a visited_count parameter. At each step, after marking a cell visited, if at target and visited_count == total, increment a global counter. Otherwise, recurse to neighbors. Key insight: The visited_count must equal total non-obstacle cells, not just any number. This prevents counting partial paths. Time: O(4^k) for paths of length k. Space: O(k) for recursion stack. Connection to L1: This is the logic inside the backtrack function. Connection to L3: This builds on basic backtracking by adding counting logic. Common mistakes: Comparing visited_count to total incorrectly (e.g., using > or <), not resetting visited_count properly on recursion. Real code pattern: def backtrack(i,j,visited_count): if invalid: return; visited[i][j]=True; visited_count+=1; if (i,j)==target and visited_count==total: count+=1; else: for neighbor in neighbors: backtrack(ni,nj,visited_count); visited[i][j]=False.

examples.input: 3x3 grid all empty, start=(0,0), target=(2,2), total=9
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at (0,0), visited_count=1. Not at target or total. Step 2: Explore neighbors. Suppose go to (0,1), visited_count=2. Continue exploring. If reach (2,2) with visited_count=5, don't count because not total 9. Only if path like (0,0)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(1,2)->(2,2) visited_count=5, invalid. A valid path must visit all 9 cells, e.g., a snake-like path covering entire grid ending at (2,2). When such path is found, count increments.

Step 1: Start (0,0), count=1. Step 2: Options: go to (0,1) or (1,0). Try (0,1): count=2. Then from (0,1), go to (1,1): count=3 but at target with count=3 not 4, so no count. Backtrack, try from (0,1) go to (1,1) later after visiting (1,0). Path (0,0)->(0,1)->(1,1)->(1,0) ends at (1,0) not target. To end at (1,1) with count=4, path must be (0,0)->(1,0)->(0,0) invalid revisit. Actually no path visits all 4 cells ending at (1,1) in 2x2? Let's check: possible path (0,0)->(1,0)->(1,1)->(0,1) ends at (0,1) not target. Another (0,0)->(0,1)->(1,1)->(1,0) ends at (1,0). So count=0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Basic Backtracking on Grid
ladders.desc: Implement simple backtracking to explore all paths without counting, just traversal.
ladders.explanation: This building block implements basic backtracking depth-first search on a grid without the additional counting constraints. The intuition is to recursively visit all reachable cells in a grid, marking visited cells to avoid cycles, and unmarking them when backtracking to explore all possible simple paths (paths without revisiting cells). This is like exploring every route in a maze without worrying about visiting all cells or reaching a specific target with a count. The algorithm: function explore(i,j): 1) Check if cell is within bounds, not obstacle, not visited. If invalid, return. 2) Mark cell as visited. 3) Recursively call explore on all four neighbors. 4) Unmark cell before returning. This will generate all possible paths from start, but doesn't count them or check for completion conditions. Time: O(4^(m*n)) in worst case. Space: O(m*n) for recursion and visited. Connection to L2: This is the foundation that L2 extends by adding counting logic. Connection to L4: This uses cell marking/unmarking operations. Common mistakes: Not handling obstacles, infinite recursion due to not marking visited. Real code pattern: def explore(i,j): if i<0 or i>=m or j<0 or j>=n or grid[i][j]==-1 or visited[i][j]: return; visited[i][j]=True; for di,dj in directions: explore(i+di,j+dj); visited[i][j]=False.

examples.trace: Step 1: explore(0,0). Mark (0,0). Step 2: Explore neighbors: down to (1,0) valid, right to (0,1) valid. Step 3: Branch explore(1,0). Mark (1,0). Explore neighbors: down out, up to (0,0) visited, right to (1,1) valid, left out. Step 4: explore(1,1). Mark (1,1). Explore: down out, up to (0,1) valid, left to (1,0) visited, right out. Step 5: explore(0,1). Mark (0,1). Explore: down to (1,1) visited, up out, left to (0,0) visited, right out. Then unmark and backtrack. Similarly other branches. This explores all simple paths like (0,0)->(1,0)->(1,1)->(0,1) etc.
Step 1: explore(0,0). Mark (0,0). Step 2: Explore neighbors: down to (1,0) obstacle, so invalid; up out; left/right out of bounds. So no further recursion. Unmark (0,0). Done.

testCases.expected: All paths explored (no output, just traversal)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Mark and Unmark Cells in Grid
ladders.desc: Implement functions to mark a cell as visited and unmark it, with bounds checking.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations of marking and unmarking cells in a grid during traversal, which is essential for backtracking. The intuition is that when exploring paths, we need to track which cells have been visited to avoid cycles. Marking a cell indicates we're currently using it in the current path; unmarking it allows other paths to use it later. This is like placing and removing a token on a board game. The algorithm involves: 1) A visited grid (2D boolean array) initialized to False. 2) Function mark(i,j): Check if i and j are within grid bounds (0<=i<m, 0<=j<n). If out of bounds, return error or false. If in bounds, set visited[i][j] = True. 3) Function unmark(i,j): Similarly check bounds, then set visited[i][j] = False. Additionally, we need to check for obstacles: before marking, ensure grid[i][j] != -1 (or whatever represents obstacle). Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(m*n) for visited grid. Connection to L3: These operations are used in the backtracking function to manage state. Connection to L5: This builds on accessing 2D arrays. Common mistakes: Not checking bounds leading to index errors, not handling obstacle cells. Real code pattern: visited = [[False]*n for _ in range(m)]; def mark(i,j): if 0<=i<m and 0<=j<n and grid[i][j]!=-1: visited[i][j]=True; return True; else: return False; def unmark(i,j): if 0<=i<m and 0<=j<n: visited[i][j]=False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize visited 3x3 all False. Step 2: mark(0,0): check bounds 0<=0<3 and 0<=0<3, true; check obstacle (assume grid[0][0]=0), true; set visited[0][0]=True. Visited: [[True,False,False],[False,False,False],[False,False,False]]. Step 3: mark(2,2): bounds ok, set visited[2][2]=True. Visited: [[True,False,False],[False,False,False],[False,False,True]]. Step 4: unmark(0,0): bounds ok, set visited[0][0]=False. Visited: [[False,False,False],[False,False,False],[False,False,True]].

examples.input: 2x2 grid, mark (0,0), mark (0,2) out of bounds
examples.trace: Step 1: visited 2x2 all False. Step 2: mark(0,0): bounds 0<=0<2 and 0<=0<2, true; set visited[0][0]=True. Step 3: mark(0,2): check bounds, j=2 not <2, so return false or skip. Visited remains [[True,False],[False,False]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Grid Representation and Neighbor Access
ladders.desc: Understand 2D grid representation and how to access adjacent cells.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for working with grid-based problems. A grid is represented as a 2D array (list of lists in Python), where each cell has coordinates (row, column). Rows are indexed from top to bottom (0 to m-1), columns left to right (0 to n-1). To access a cell, use grid[i][j] where i is row index, j is column index. Adjacent cells (neighbors) are accessed by adding offsets: up (i-1, j), down (i+1, j), left (i, j-1), right (i, j+1). It's crucial to check bounds before accessing to avoid index errors. The intuition is that a grid is like a chessboard; moving to neighbors means changing row or column by ±1. This is basic matrix traversal. Step-by-step: 1) Define grid dimensions m (rows) and n (columns). 2) To check if (i,j) is within bounds: 0 <= i < m and 0 <= j < n. 3) To get neighbor coordinates: create list of direction vectors [(1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)] and add to current (i,j). 4) For each neighbor, check bounds before accessing. Time: O(1) per access. Space: O(1) for coordinates. Connection to L4: This is used to determine which cells to mark/unmark. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in bounds, confusing rows and columns. Real code pattern: m=len(grid); n=len(grid[0]); directions = [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]; for di,dj in directions: ni, nj = i+di, j+dj; if 0<=ni<m and 0<=nj<n: # access grid[ni][nj].

examples.trace: Step 1: Grid has m=3, n=3. Current cell (i,j)=(1,1). Step 2: Directions: (1,0) gives (2,1) - down, within bounds (2<3,1<3). (-1,0) gives (0,1) - up, within bounds. (0,1) gives (1,2) - right, within bounds. (0,-1) gives (1,0) - left, within bounds. So all four neighbors are valid.

Step 1: m=2,n=2. Cell (0,0). Step 2: Directions: (1,0) gives (1,0) - down, valid. (-1,0) gives (-1,0) - up, invalid since -1<0. (0,1) gives (0,1) - right, valid. (0,-1) gives (0,-1) - left, invalid. So valid neighbors: down and right.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Backtracking with Bitmask O(m*n*2^(m*n))
approaches.summary: Use backtracking with a bitmask to track visited cells, reducing redundant checks by memoizing states based on position and visited set. This avoids re-exploring the same partial paths.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Backtracking with Bitmask for Unique Paths III
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting all unique paths covering every non-obstacle cell exactly once using backtracking with bitmask memoization.
ladders.explanation: The Unique Paths III problem requires counting all paths from a start cell to an end cell that visit every non-obstacle cell exactly once in a grid with obstacles. The backtracking with bitmask approach solves this by exploring all possible paths while tracking visited cells efficiently using bitmask representation. Intuition: We need to explore all possible paths (backtracking) but avoid redundant exploration of the same state (position + visited cells), which we achieve by memoizing states using a bitmask. Each cell is assigned a bit position in an integer mask (1 << cell_index). When we visit a cell, we set its bit; when we backtrack, we unset it. We use memoization (dp dictionary) to store results for (position, mask) pairs to avoid recomputation. The algorithm: 1. Preprocess grid to count total non-obstacle cells and find start/end positions. 2. Define recursive DFS function that takes current position (r,c) and visited mask. 3. Base cases: if out of bounds or obstacle or cell already visited, return 0; if at end position, return 1 if all non-obstacle cells visited (mask has all bits set), else 0. 4. Mark current cell visited by setting its bit in mask. 5. Explore four directions recursively, summing results. 6. Unmark current cell (backtrack) for other paths. 7. Memoize result in dp before returning. Time complexity: O(m*n*2^(m*n)) as there are m*n positions and 2^(m*n) possible masks. Space: O(m*n*2^(m*n)) for dp storage. Connection to L1: This integrates the DFS with bitmask subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting to unmark cells during backtracking, incorrect bitmask operations, not checking all non-obstacle cells visited at end. Real code pattern: Use dictionary for dp, bit operations for mask updates, recursion with backtracking.

examples.trace: Grid 3x4. Total non-obstacle cells: 1 (start) + 10 (empty) + 1 (end) = 12. Start at (0,0), end at (2,2). Initialize mask=0, set start bit: mask=1<<(0*4+0)=1. DFS from (0,0): Explore down to (1,0): mask=1|(1<<4)=17. Continue exploring... First path: (0,0)->(1,0)->(2,0)->(2,1)->(2,2)->(2,3)->(1,3)->(1,2)->(1,1)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(0,3). At end (2,2), mask has all 12 bits set, count=1. Second path: (0,0)->(0,1)->(0,2)->(0,3)->(1,3)->(1,2)->(1,1)->(1,0)->(2,0)->(2,1)->(2,2). At end, mask set, count=2. Total=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS with Bitmask for Path Counting
ladders.desc: Recursive DFS function that explores paths from a position with visited mask, returning count of valid paths to end.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core recursive function that implements backtracking with bitmask. Intuition: From any cell, we need to count all paths to the end that cover all required cells, given which cells we've already visited. We use recursion to explore all directions, and bitmask to efficiently represent visited cells as an integer. Step-by-step: 1. Function dfs(r,c,mask) returns number of valid paths from (r,c) to end given visited mask. 2. Check base cases: if out of bounds or grid[r][c]==-1 (obstacle), return 0. 3. Calculate cell_index = r*cols + c. If mask & (1<<cell_index) != 0 (already visited), return 0. 4. Update mask = mask | (1<<cell_index). 5. If (r,c) is end cell: return 1 if mask == all_ones (all non-obstacle bits set), else 0. 6. Otherwise, initialize count=0. For each direction (up,down,left,right): count += dfs(nr,nc,mask). 7. Return count. Note: We don't unmark mask here because mask is passed by value (copied), so backtracking is automatic. Time: O(4^L) without memoization where L is path length, but with memoization becomes O(m*n*2^(m*n)). Space: O(m*n) recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called from main solution. Common mistakes: Not checking all non-obstacle cells visited at end, incorrect bit operations. Real code pattern: Recursive function with bitmask parameter, base cases, directional loops.

examples.input: grid=[[1,0],[0,2]], start=(0,0), end=(1,1), all_ones=3 (binary 11), call dfs(0,0,0)
examples.trace: dfs(0,0,0): cell_index=0, mask=0|1=1. Not end. Explore: down to (1,0): dfs(1,0,1): cell_index=2, mask=1|4=5. Not end. Explore: right to (1,1): dfs(1,1,5): cell_index=3, mask=5|8=13. At end, mask=13 != 3, return 0. Back to (1,0): no more moves, return 0. Back to (0,0): right to (0,1): dfs(0,1,1): cell_index=1, mask=1|2=3. Not end. Explore: down to (1,1): dfs(1,1,3): cell_index=3, mask=3|8=11. At end, mask=11 != 3, return 0. No more moves, return 0. Total count=0? Wait, correct logic: all_ones should be mask with bits for all non-obstacle cells: cells (0,0)=start, (0,1)=empty, (1,0)=empty, (1,1)=end → bits 0,1,2,3 all set = 15. Let's recompute: all_ones=15. dfs(0,0,0): mask=1. dfs(1,0,1): mask=1|4=5. dfs(1,1,5): mask=5|8=13. At end, mask=13 !=15, return 0. dfs(0,1,1): mask=1|2=3. dfs(1,1,3): mask=3|8=11. At end, mask=11 !=15, return 0. Total=0. But expected 1 path? Actually, grid has 4 cells all non-obstacle, so path must visit all 4: (0,0)->(0,1)->(1,1)->(1,0) is invalid because end at (1,1) not last. Correct path: (0,0)->(1,0)->(1,1)->(0,1) covers all but end not last. Actually, problem requires end last, so path must be (0,0)->(0,1)->(1,1) doesn't cover all. Let's use example from L0: grid [[1,0],[0,2]] has 3 non-obstacle? Start (0,0), empty (0,1), end (1,1), obstacle? No, all 0 or 1 or 2. So 3 cells: bits 0,1,3 set = 11. all_ones=11. dfs(0,0,0): mask=1. Explore down: dfs(1,0,1): mask=1|4=5. Not end, explore right: dfs(1,1,5): mask=5|8=13. At end, mask=13 !=11, return 0. Explore right from start: dfs(0,1,1): mask=1|2=3. Explore down: dfs(1,1,3): mask=3|8=11. At end, mask=11==11, return 1. Total=1. Correct.
dfs(0,0,0): mask=1. Explore directions... This traces one path as in L0 example, eventually returning 4 after exploring all. Detailed trace similar to L0 but for subroutine call.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Visited Cells
ladders.desc: Using integer bitmask to efficiently track set of visited cells in grid.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is representing which cells have been visited as a bitmask—an integer where each bit corresponds to a cell in the grid. Intuition: Instead of storing a visited set or array, we use a single integer, making state representation compact and hashable for memoization. Each cell at position (r,c) is assigned a unique index: index = r * cols + c. We use 1 << index to represent that cell in the mask. Operations: Mark cell visited: mask = mask | (1 << index). Check if visited: (mask & (1 << index)) != 0. Unmark: mask = mask & ~(1 << index). This allows O(1) time for visit checks and updates. Why it works: For a grid with m*n cells, there are 2^(m*n) possible visited sets, each representable by a unique integer from 0 to 2^(m*n)-1. This enables memoization using dictionary with key (position, mask). Step-by-step: 1. Initialize mask=0 (no cells visited). 2. When visiting cell i, set bit: mask |= (1<<i). 3. When backtracking, clear bit: mask &= ~(1<<i). 4. To check if all required cells visited, compare mask to a precomputed all_ones mask where bits for all non-obstacle cells are set. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1) for mask storage. Connection to L1: This technique is used in the DFS subroutine to pass visited state. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in index calculation, forgetting to use bitwise operations correctly, not handling large grids where mask may exceed integer size (but in practice, m*n <= 20 for this problem). Real code pattern: Use integer variable mask, bitwise OR, AND, left shift.

examples.input: Grid 2x2, cells: (0,0) index 0, (0,1) index 1, (1,0) index 2, (1,1) index 3. Start with mask=0. Visit (0,0).
examples.trace: Initial mask=0. Cell (0,0) index=0. mask = 0 | (1<<0) = 0 | 1 = 1. Binary: 0001. Bit 0 set.

examples.input: Same grid, mask=1. Visit (1,1). Then check if (0,1) visited.
examples.trace: Current mask=1 (binary 0001). Visit (1,1): index=3. mask = 1 | (1<<3) = 1 | 8 = 9. Binary: 1001. Check (0,1): index=1. (mask & (1<<1)) = (9 & 2) = 0, so not visited.

testCases.input: rows=2,cols=2, mask=6, are all cells (0,1) and (1,0) visited?

ladders.title: Building Block — Backtracking Exploration on Grid
ladders.desc: Recursively exploring all possible paths in a grid with constraints.
ladders.explanation: Backtracking is a systematic way to explore all possible paths or configurations by making choices, recursing, and undoing choices (backtracking). Intuition: In a grid, from each cell, we can move in up to 4 directions. We need to explore all sequences of moves that satisfy constraints (e.g., not revisiting cells, covering all cells). Backtracking does this by: 1. At current cell, mark it visited. 2. For each possible next cell (neighbors), if valid (within bounds, not obstacle, not visited), recursively explore from there. 3. After exploring all neighbors, unmark current cell (backtrack) so it can be used in other paths. This ensures all permutations of paths are considered. Step-by-step algorithm: function backtrack(r,c): if base_case_met: update result; return. mark (r,c) as visited. for each direction (dr,dc) in [(1,0),(-1,0),(0,1),(0,-1)]: nr, nc = r+dr, c+dc. if isValid(nr,nc) and not visited[nr][nc]: backtrack(nr,nc). unmark (r,c) as visited. Time complexity: O(4^N) where N is number of cells in worst case, as each step has up to 4 choices. Space: O(N) for recursion depth and visited storage. Connection to L2: Backtracking provides the exploration framework; bitmask optimizes visited tracking. Common mistakes: Forgetting to unmark cells (leads to incomplete exploration), not handling base cases correctly, infinite recursion if visited not checked. Real code pattern: Recursive function with visited array, directional loop, backtracking step.

examples.input: Grid 2x2 all empty, start (0,0), end (1,1), must visit all cells. Use backtracking without bitmask.
examples.trace: Initialize visited all false. backtrack(0,0): mark (0,0) visited. Explore: down to (1,0): backtrack(1,0): mark visited. Explore: right to (1,1): backtrack(1,1): mark visited. All cells visited, end reached, count=1. Backtrack: unmark (1,1), return. From (1,0): no more moves, unmark (1,0), return. From (0,0): right to (0,1): backtrack(0,1): mark visited. Explore: down to (1,1): backtrack(1,1): mark visited. All visited, count=2. Unmark and return. Total paths=2.

examples.input: Grid 1x3: start (0,0), end (0,2), must visit all. Backtracking.
examples.trace: backtrack(0,0): mark visited. Explore right to (0,1): backtrack(0,1): mark visited. Explore right to (0,2): backtrack(0,2): mark visited. All visited, count=1. Backtrack. Total=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Grid Traversal with Directional Moves
ladders.desc: Moving through a grid by stepping to adjacent cells in four directions.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves navigating a 2D grid by moving from a cell to its neighbors: up, down, left, right. Intuition: A grid is represented as a 2D array with rows and columns. From any cell (r,c), the adjacent cells are at (r-1,c), (r+1,c), (r,c-1), (r,c+1), provided they are within grid boundaries. This is fundamental for path exploration problems. Step-by-step: 1. Define directions as list of (dr,dc) pairs: [(1,0), (-1,0), (0,1), (0,-1)]. 2. For current (r,c), loop through directions: nr = r + dr, nc = c + dc. 3. Check if (nr,nc) is within bounds: 0 <= nr < rows and 0 <= nc < cols. 4. If valid, process cell (e.g., move to it). This ensures we only consider moves that stay in the grid. Time: O(1) per direction check, O(4) per cell. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in backtracking to generate next possible cells. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bounds checking, forgetting to handle all four directions, incorrect direction order. Real code pattern: for dr,dc in directions: nr,nc = r+dr, c+dc; if 0<=nr<rows and 0<=nc<cols: ...

examples.input: Grid 3x3, current cell (1,1). List all valid neighbors.
examples.trace: Rows=3, cols=3. Current (r,c)=(1,1). Directions: (1,0) -> (2,1) valid. (-1,0) -> (0,1) valid. (0,1) -> (1,2) valid. (0,-1) -> (1,0) valid. All within 0<=r<3, 0<=c<3.

examples.input: Grid 2x2, current cell (0,0). List valid neighbors.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Grid Representation and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding how a 2D grid is stored and accessed using row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: A grid is a 2D array of cells, often represented in programming as a list of lists (e.g., in Python: grid = [[...], [...], ...]). Each inner list represents a row, and elements within represent columns. Intuition: We use two indices: row index (r) and column index (c) to access a specific cell: grid[r][c]. Row indices start at 0 for the top row and increase downward. Column indices start at 0 for the leftmost column and increase rightward. This is like coordinates in a matrix. Why it matters: For path problems, we need to track positions and move between cells. Step-by-step: 1. Grid has 'rows' number of rows and 'cols' number of columns. 2. Valid indices: 0 <= r < rows, 0 <= c < cols. 3. To convert 2D position (r,c) to a flat index (useful for bitmask): index = r * cols + c. This assigns a unique number to each cell by row-major order. 4. To convert flat index back to 2D: r = index // cols, c = index % cols. Time: O(1) for access and conversion. Space: O(rows*cols) for grid storage. Connection to L4: This foundation is needed to define moves and positions. Common mistakes: Confusing rows and columns, off-by-one errors, incorrect flat index calculation. Real code pattern: Use nested loops for traversal, grid[r][c] for access.

examples.input: Grid with 2 rows, 3 columns: grid = [[a,b,c],[d,e,f]]. Access cell at row 1, column 2.
examples.trace: Rows are 0-indexed: row 0 is [a,b,c], row 1 is [d,e,f]. Columns 0-indexed: column 0 is first element, column 1 second, column 2 third. So grid[1][2] = f.

examples.input: Same grid, flat index for cell (1,1).

approaches.name: Approach 3: Dynamic Programming with Bitmask O(m*n*2^(m*n))
approaches.summary: Apply dynamic programming using a bitmask to represent visited cells, iteratively building up solutions from smaller states. This avoids recursion overhead and systematically computes paths.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Counting Unique Paths with DP and Bitmask
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming with bitmask to count all unique paths covering every non-obstacle cell exactly once.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Unique Paths III problem by modeling the grid traversal as a state-based dynamic programming problem. The key insight is that we can represent which cells have been visited using a bitmask, where each bit corresponds to a cell in the grid. The DP state dp[r][c][mask] stores the number of ways to reach cell (r,c) having visited exactly the cells indicated by the bitmask. We start from the starting cell with only that cell visited, then iteratively expand to neighboring cells, adding them to the visited mask. The solution is the sum of dp values at the ending cell with the full mask (all non-obstacle cells visited). The intuition is that we're systematically building up all possible paths by considering each possible visited state, avoiding recursion overhead while ensuring we count each valid path exactly once. The algorithm works by initializing dp[start_r][start_c][1<<start_idx] = 1, then for each mask from 1 to (1<<n)-1 (where n is number of non-obstacle cells), for each cell (r,c), if dp[r][c][mask] > 0, we try moving to each valid neighbor (up, down, left, right) that is within bounds, not an obstacle, and not already visited in the current mask. For each valid move to (nr,nc), we update dp[nr][nc][mask | (1<<cell_idx(nr,nc))] += dp[r][c][mask]. Finally, we sum dp[end_r][end_c][full_mask] where full_mask has bits set for all non-obstacle cells. Time complexity is O(m*n*2^(m*n)) as we iterate over all cells and all possible masks. Space complexity is O(m*n*2^(m*n)) for the DP table. Common mistakes include incorrect bitmask handling (off-by-one in bit positions), not handling obstacles correctly in the mask calculation, and missing the requirement to visit ALL non-obstacle cells exactly once. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the bitmask state transition as its core mechanism.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Grid has 3 rows, 4 columns. Non-obstacle cells: all except obstacles (-1). Starting cell (0,0), ending cell (2,2). Total non-obstacle cells: 11 (all cells except the -1 at (2,3)).
Step 2: Initialize DP table size 3×4×2^11. dp[0][0][1<<0] = 1 (cell index 0 for (0,0)).
Step 3: Iterate masks from 1 to 2047. For mask=1 (only start visited): From (0,0), valid moves: right to (0,1) (index 1) and down to (1,0) (index 4). Update dp[0][1][1|(1<<1)=3] += 1, dp[1][0][1|(1<<4)=17] += 1.
Step 4: Continue processing all masks. Key paths develop: (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(0,3)→(1,3)→(1,2)→(1,1)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,1)→(2,2) and (0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,1)→(1,1)→(1,2)→(0,2)→(0,3)→(1,3)→(0,3)→(0,2)→(0,1)→(1,1)→(2,1)→(2,2) (simplified).
Step 5: At mask=2047 (all 11 bits set), dp[2][2][2047] = 2. Return 2.

Step 1: 3×3 grid, all cells non-obstacle. Start (0,0), end (2,2). Total cells: 9.
Step 2: Initialize DP 3×3×2^9. dp[0][0][1] = 1.
Step 3: Process masks. Example path: (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(1,1)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,1)→(2,2). Another: (0,0)→(1,0)→(2,0)→(2,1)→(1,1)→(0,1)→(0,2)→(1,2)→(2,2).
Step 4: At full mask 511 (binary 111111111), dp[2][2][511] accumulates to 4.
Step 5: Return 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Bitmask State Transition for Grid DP
ladders.desc: Implementing the DP state transition using bitmask to track visited cells.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the heart of the full solution: transitioning between DP states based on cell movements and visited masks. The core idea is that each state (r,c,mask) represents being at cell (r,c) having visited the cells indicated by the bitmask. From this state, we can move to any neighboring cell (nr,nc) that is valid (within grid, not obstacle, not already visited). The new state becomes (nr,nc, new_mask) where new_mask = mask | (1 << idx(nr,nc)), and we add the count from the current state to the new state. The intuition is that we're building up paths incrementally: each transition adds one more cell to the visited set. The algorithm: 1. Precompute mapping from cell coordinates to bit indices (0-based). 2. Initialize DP table with all zeros. 3. Set dp[start_r][start_c][1<<start_idx] = 1. 4. For each mask from 1 to (1<<n)-1 (in increasing order), for each cell (r,c), if dp[r][c][mask] > 0: for each neighbor (nr,nc) in four directions: if valid and not visited in mask: new_mask = mask | (1<<idx(nr,nc)); dp[nr][nc][new_mask] += dp[r][c][mask]. 5. The result is dp[end_r][end_c][full_mask]. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(m*n*2^n * 4) but dominated by the mask iteration. Space is O(m*n*2^n). Common mistakes: incorrect bit indexing (off-by-one), not checking obstacles before bit operations, iterating masks in wrong order (must be increasing to avoid reusing states). This feeds into L0 as the main computational engine.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Map cells: (0,0)→0, (0,1)→1, (1,0)→2, (1,1)→3. n=4.
Step 2: Initialize dp[0][0][1]=1.
Step 3: mask=1 (binary 0001): From (0,0), moves: right to (0,1) (valid, idx=1). new_mask=1|(1<<1)=3 (0011). dp[0][1][3]=1. Down to (1,0) (idx=2). new_mask=1|(1<<2)=5 (0101). dp[1][0][5]=1.
Step 4: mask=3 (0011): From (0,1), moves: left to (0,0) (already visited in mask, skip). Down to (1,1) (idx=3, valid). new_mask=3|(1<<3)=11 (1011). dp[1][1][11]=1.
Step 5: mask=5 (0101): From (1,0), moves: up to (0,0) (visited, skip). Right to (1,1) (idx=3). new_mask=5|(1<<3)=13 (1101). dp[1][1][13]=1.
Step 6: Continue other masks. Full mask=15 (1111). dp[1][1][15] = dp[1][1][11] + dp[1][1][13] = 2.

Step 1: n=9. Start (0,0) idx=0.
Step 2: mask=1: From (0,0), moves to (0,1) idx=1: new_mask=3, dp[0][1][3]=1; to (1,0) idx=3: new_mask=9, dp[1][0][9]=1.
Step 3: mask=3: From (0,1), moves to (0,0) (visited, skip), to (0,2) idx=2: new_mask=7, dp[0][2][7]=1; to (1,1) idx=4: new_mask=19, dp[1][1][19]=1.
Step 4: This continues building all paths.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Visited Cells
ladders.desc: Using bitmask to compactly represent which cells have been visited in a path.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is representing the set of visited cells using a bitmask integer. Each cell in the grid is assigned a unique index (0 to n-1, where n is the number of non-obstacle cells). A bitmask is an integer where the i-th bit is 1 if cell i has been visited, 0 otherwise. For example, if we have 4 cells, mask 5 (binary 0101) means cells 0 and 2 are visited. This allows O(1) operations to check if a cell is visited (mask & (1<<idx) != 0), add a cell to visited set (mask | (1<<idx)), and check if all cells are visited (mask == full_mask where full_mask = (1<<n)-1). The intuition is that we can encode the entire visitation state in a single integer, making DP feasible. The algorithm for this logic: 1. Assign indices to non-obstacle cells (e.g., row-major order skipping obstacles). 2. For any state, to move to cell with index i: if (mask & (1<<i)) == 0 (not visited), new_mask = mask | (1<<i). 3. The full solution is found when mask has all n bits set. Time complexity for mask operations is O(1) per operation. Space for storing masks is O(2^n) possibilities. Common mistakes: incorrect indexing (mixing obstacle and non-obstacle cells), not using 1<<idx (using idx directly), misunderstanding bit ordering. This feeds into L1 as the fundamental state representation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: mask=3 means cells 0 and 1 visited (bits 0 and 1 set).
Step 2: Check if cell 2 visited: mask & (1<<2) = 3 & 4 = 0 → not visited.
Step 3: Add cell 2: new_mask = 3 | (1<<2) = 3 | 4 = 7 (binary 0111).
Step 4: Check if cell 0 visited: mask & (1<<0) = 3 & 1 = 1 → visited.
Step 5: Full mask for 4 cells: (1<<4)-1 = 15 (1111). Current mask 7 != 15 → not all visited.

Step 1: mask=1: cell 0 visited.
Step 2: Add cell 1: mask = 1 | (1<<1) = 1 | 2 = 3 (0011).
Step 3: Add cell 2: mask = 3 | (1<<2) = 3 | 4 = 7 (0111).
Step 4: Full mask = (1<<3)-1 = 7. Reached full mask.

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal with Constraints
ladders.desc: Moving through a grid while respecting boundaries and obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves navigating a 2D grid from a start to end cell, moving only up, down, left, or right, while avoiding obstacles and staying within grid boundaries. The intuition is that we need to systematically explore all possible paths subject to these constraints. The algorithm: 1. Define grid dimensions m rows, n columns. 2. Define movement directions: [(0,1), (0,-1), (1,0), (-1,0)]. 3. From any cell (r,c), for each direction (dr,dc): new cell (nr,nc) = (r+dr, c+dc). 4. Check validity: 0 <= nr < m, 0 <= nc < n, and grid[nr][nc] != -1 (not obstacle). 5. If valid, proceed to that cell. This forms the basis for path exploration. Time complexity depends on number of paths; in worst case exponential. Space for recursion or queue is O(m*n). Common mistakes: forgetting to check grid bounds, incorrect obstacle value, not marking visited cells (but here visited is handled by bitmask). This feeds into L2 as the physical movement mechanism.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Grid 3×3, obstacle at (1,1). Start (0,0).
Step 2: Directions: right (0,1)→ (0,1): valid (within bounds, not obstacle).
Step 3: down (1,0)→ (1,0): valid.
Step 4: left (0,-1)→ (0,-1): invalid (out of bounds).
Step 5: up (-1,0)→ (-1,0): invalid.
Step 6: From (0,0), valid neighbors: (0,1) and (1,0).

Step 1: Grid 2×2, no obstacles.
Step 2: From (0,0): move right to (0,1).
Step 3: From (0,1): move down to (1,1).
Step 4: From (1,1): move left to (1,0).
Step 5: From (1,0): move up to (0,0).
Step 6: This forms a cycle covering all cells.

testCases.input: grid=[[0,0],[0,0]], start=(0,0), number of paths to (1,1) without revisiting
grid=[[0,0,0],[0,0,0]], start=(0,0), traverse all cells exactly once possible?

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bit Manipulation for Set Operations
ladders.desc: Using bitwise operations to implement set membership and updates.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using bitwise operators to manipulate sets represented as bitmasks. Key operations: 1. Setting a bit: mask | (1 << i) — sets the i-th bit to 1. 2. Checking a bit: (mask & (1 << i)) != 0 — returns true if i-th bit is 1. 3. Toggling a bit: mask ^ (1 << i) — flips the i-th bit. 4. Clearing a bit: mask & ~(1 << i) — sets i-th bit to 0. 5. Full set: (1 << n) - 1 — creates a mask with all n bits set to 1. The intuition is that integers in binary can represent subsets of {0,1,...,n-1}, with bitwise operations providing efficient O(1) set operations. For example, if n=3, mask 5 (101) represents set {0,2}. Algorithm: To add element 1: mask = 5 | (1<<1) = 5 | 2 = 7 (111). To check element 2: (5 & (1<<2)) = (5 & 4) = 4 != 0 → true. Time and space are O(1). Common mistakes: using 1 << i with i >= 64 causing overflow, misunderstanding bit order (LSB is bit 0), not using parentheses correctly. This feeds into L3 as the tool for tracking visited cells.

examples.trace:
Step 1: mask=6 represents set {1,2} (bits 1 and 2 set).
Step 2: Check bit 0: (6 & (1<<0)) = 6 & 1 = 0 → false.
Step 3: Check bit 1: (6 & (1<<1)) = 6 & 2 = 2 → true.
Step 4: Add bit 3: mask = 6 | (1<<3) = 6 | 8 = 14 (1110).
Step 5: Remove bit 2: mask = 6 & ~(1<<2) = 6 & ~4 = 6 & 11 = 2 (0010).

examples.input: n=3, start with empty set, add elements 0,2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initial mask=0 (000).
Step 2: Add element 0: mask = 0 | (1<<0) = 1 (001).
Step 3: Add element 2: mask = 1 | (1<<2) = 1 | 4 = 5 (101).
Step 4: Check element 1: (5 & (1<<1)) = 5 & 2 = 0 → false.
Step 5: Full set: (1<<3)-1 = 7 (111).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bitwise Operators
ladders.desc: Understanding binary representation and basic bitwise operations.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: representing numbers in binary and using bitwise operators. Binary is base-2, using digits 0 and 1. Each bit position represents a power of 2 (rightmost is 2^0=1, next 2^1=2, etc.). For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1). Bitwise operators work on these bits: 1. AND (&): 1 & 1 = 1, otherwise 0. E.g., 5 & 3 = 101 & 011 = 001 = 1. 2. OR (|): 1 | 1 = 1, 1 | 0 = 1, 0 | 0 = 0. E.g., 5 | 3 = 101 | 011 = 111 = 7. 3. XOR (^): 1 ^ 1 = 0, 1 ^ 0 = 1, 0 ^ 0 = 0. E.g., 5 ^ 3 = 101 ^ 011 = 110 = 6. 4. NOT (~): flips bits, but careful with signed integers. 5. Left shift (<<): shifts bits left, filling with 0. E.g., 1 << 2 = 4 (binary 100). 6. Right shift (>>): shifts bits right. Intuition: these allow manipulating individual bits efficiently. Time and space are O(1). Common mistakes: confusing bitwise AND with logical AND, not understanding two's complement for negative numbers, shift overflow. This feeds into L4 as the mathematical basis.
